findings.title: Check-then-create race in _experiment_id loses the whole experiment run under concurrent first-time start_run
findings.description: MlflowExperimentTracker._experiment_id() does get_experiment_by_name() then, if None, create_experiment() — a classic TOCTOU. The first time a given experiment name is used, two concurrent start_run() calls can both observe get_experiment_by_name() == None and both call create_experiment(); MLflow rejects the duplicate with MlflowException(RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS) for the loser. That exception propagates out of _experiment_id -> start_run. This is reachable under the engine's own concurrency model: the reasoning seam runs an 8-thread pool, and multiple study processes legitimately share one local store (SQLite/file backend), so the create can race across threads and across processes. The consequence is exactly the lifecycle failure the rest of this module is engineered to avoid: in run_study (pipeline.py:351-370) a start_run exception is caught and leaves run_id=None, after which every track() call silently no-ops, so ALL metrics, the run_duration SLO metric, and the provenance-trail artifact correlation are dropped for that entire study run, and no MLflow run exists at all. The module docstring and start_run's #F-023 orphan-termination logic explicitly target phantom/dropped runs, but the experiment-creation step itself is unguarded, and there is no test for the duplicate-create path (tests only mock get_experiment_by_name returning None with a non-raising create_experiment).
findings.poc:
import threading
from assay_engine.observability.tracking import MlflowExperimentTracker
# Shared local store, brand-new experiment name (so get_experiment_by_name == None for all).
# Two threads (mirrors the reasoning pool / two study processes) race the first start_run:
tracker = MlflowExperimentTracker(experiment='race-demo')
errors = []
def go():
    try:
        tracker.start_run('r', {})
    except Exception as e:  # one thread raises MlflowException(RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS)
        errors.append(e)
ts = [threading.Thread(target=go) for _ in range(2)]
[t.start() for t in ts]; [t.join() for t in ts]
assert errors, 'expected one start_run to lose the create_experiment race'
# In run_study this surfaces as run_id=None -> all metrics for the study silently dropped.
findings.suggested_fix: Make experiment resolution race-tolerant: wrap create_experiment in a try/except that, on the duplicate-name error (MlflowException with error_code RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS, or more robustly any failure), re-fetches via get_experiment_by_name(self._experiment) and returns its experiment_id; only re-raise if the re-fetch still returns None. This mirrors the get-or-create idiom MLflow's own mlflow.set_experiment uses and removes the window. Add a test that simulates create_experiment raising the already-exists error and asserts _experiment_id falls back to the now-present experiment.

findings.title: Build backend (hatchling) fetched live and un-hashed during `pip install -e .`, defeating the reproducibility/hash-pin guarantee
findings.description: All three jobs install the package with `pip install --no-deps -e .` (lines 35, 80, 191). `--no-deps` suppresses runtime dependency resolution but does NOT disable PEP 517 build isolation. Because pyproject.toml declares `build-system.requires = ["hatchling"]` (pyproject.toml:1-3) and the version is dynamic (computed by hatchling at build time), pip creates an isolated build environment and fetches `hatchling` and its deps (`editables`, `trove-classifiers`, etc.) LIVE from PyPI, un-pinned and un-hashed, every CI run. Neither `requirements.lock` nor `requirements-core.lock` contains `hatchling`/`editables`/`trove-classifiers` (confirmed: 0 matches). Consequences: (1) The workflow's central claim — installing 'EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned and the SBOM describes' (lines 29-32, 76-77) — is false for the build toolchain; (2) pip-audit (`-r requirements.lock`) never sees hatchling, so a compromised hatchling/editables patch release executes arbitrary code during the editable build in CI yet is never scanned — the exact `#F-004` supply-chain window the comments claim is closed; (3) the SBOM omits the build-time dependency set; (4) a yanked/changed hatchling release makes a previously-green commit non-reproducible.
findings.poc: In CI on a clean runner, `pip install --no-deps -e .` triggers `Getting requirements to build editable ... Installing build dependencies: hatchling, editables, ...` resolved live from PyPI with no `--require-hashes` and no lock entry. Verify: `grep -ic '^hatchling==' requirements.lock requirements-core.lock` -> 0 for both, while the package still builds via hatchling.
findings.suggested_fix: Include the build backend in the hash-pinned set and disable live build isolation in CI. Either (a) add hatchling to the locks (e.g. `uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes` after adding hatchling to a build/dev group) and install with `pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` so the already-hashed hatchling is used; or (b) build a wheel in a separately-pinned, hashed build env and `pip install` that wheel. Also extend the SBOM/pip-audit coverage to the build toolchain.

findings.title: License gate scans entire site-packages (`--with-system`), not the hash-pinned set the comment claims
findings.description: The license-gate step comment (lines 184-189) asserts it scans 'the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited, not a live resolution.' In practice the command is `pip-licenses --format=json --with-system` (line 193), which enumerates EVERY distribution in site-packages. By that point the env also contains `pip-licenses==5.5.5` and its transitive deps (prettytable, wcwidth, etc.) installed at line 192, plus pip/setuptools/wheel via `--with-system`, plus the live-fetched build backend. So the license-checked set is a superset of `requirements.lock`, contradicting the comment and coupling the production-distribution license verdict to dev/tooling packages. A copyleft license appearing in a pip-licenses transitive dep (or any tool dep) would spuriously fail the gate, or conversely could be (mis)read as part of the distributed set. Additionally, `pip-licenses==5.5.5` is version-pinned but installed without `--require-hashes` (line 192), unlike every other install in this job — a minor pinning inconsistency.
findings.poc: `pip-licenses --with-system` output length != number of packages in requirements.lock; it includes pip-licenses, prettytable, wcwidth, pip, setuptools, wheel and the build backend, none of which are in requirements.lock.
findings.suggested_fix: Scan only the pinned distribution set: run `pip-licenses` in/against the locked environment without `--with-system`, or drive it from `requirements.lock` package names (e.g. `pip-licenses --packages $(...)`), and install pip-licenses in a separate step/venv so tooling deps never enter the scanned environment. Add `--require-hashes` (with a hash) for the pip-licenses install to match the rest of the job.

findings.title: Operator full display name (git author) is NOT scrubbed — leaks in committed transcripts (CV-O-1 class)
findings.description: The transcript scrubber (_SECRET_PATTERNS + _USERNAME_RE) redacts the operator's EMAIL and the bare home-directory USERNAME ('[REDACTED]'), and masks the username inside /Users//home paths and project-dir slugs. It does NOT redact the operator's real full DISPLAY NAME ('[REDACTED]'), which is distinct operator PII that appears throughout the transcripts via git author strings, pyproject authors=[], and prose. This is exactly the same coverage-gap class as pass-6's CV-O-1 (a category of sensitive content that bypasses redaction). The leak is real and already committed: '[REDACTED]' is present in cleartext in 5+ files under transcripts/sessions/ (e.g. the top-level .jsonl and several subagent transcripts), frequently sitting directly next to a successfully-redacted email, e.g. `{ name = "[REDACTED]", email = "[REDACTED]" }` and `Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>`. De-identifying the email while leaving the adjacent full name defeats the de-identification (the whole point of #F-024/#G-007 was to avoid publishing the operator's identity). git config user.name = '[REDACTED]' confirms this is the operator's identity, not a coincidental token. No test in tests/test_scripts.py covers full-name redaction, confirming the gap is unintentional rather than a documented scope decision.
findings.poc:
1) cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
2) grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/  -> multiple files with count >=1 (cleartext)
3) python3 -c 'import importlib.util as u; s=u.spec_from_file_location("c","scripts/capture_transcripts.py"); m=u.module_from_spec(s); s.loader.exec_module(m); print(m._scrub("Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>"))'
   -> prints: Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>   (email masked, full name leaks)
4) git config user.name -> '[REDACTED]' (confirms it is operator PII)
findings.suggested_fix: Derive the operator display name (e.g. from `git config user.name`, and/or a configurable ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES env list) and add it to the redaction set, mirroring _username_pattern(): build a re.escape'd, word-boundaried, length-guarded pattern and apply it in _scrub() (skip if empty/too-short to avoid over-redacting common words). Add a test_scripts.py case asserting `_scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <a@b.c>')` masks both the name and the email. Because the leak is already committed, also re-run capture (or scrub history) so existing transcripts/ files are remediated.

findings.title: MANIFEST.json file paths are written without passing through _scrub
findings.description: main() writes transcripts/MANIFEST.json via json.dumps() with no scrubbing. Each entry's 'path' is str(dstf.relative_to(...)) and the top-level 'active_session' is the session stem; these are derived from source filenames (rel = f.relative_to(subtree)). Today all captured filenames are opaque UUID/agent-id stems with no PII, so nothing leaks in practice — this is why it is LOW, not a live leak. However it is the same structural class as CV-O-1: a committed artifact (the MANIFEST) that bypasses the redaction path entirely. If any future transcript file in the session subtree is ever named with operator-identifying content (a path/slug/username in a filename, e.g. a workflow- or tool-result artifact named after a working dir), that PII would be copied verbatim into MANIFEST.json. The file content is scrubbed (CV-O-1 fix) but the manifest of paths is not.
findings.poc: Inspect main() lines 151-166: the dict (including captured[].path and active_session) is serialized with json.dumps() and written to MANIFEST without any _scrub() call. Contrast with _copy_scrubbed which scrubs file CONTENT but returns the raw relative path string for the manifest.
findings.suggested_fix: Run the assembled manifest dict's string fields through _scrub() before writing (e.g. scrub each rec['path'] and the 'active_session' value, or json.dumps then _scrub the serialized blob before write_text). Cheap, defense-in-depth, and closes the structural gap for future filename shapes.

findings.title: High-stakes (T3) error-classification tests are non-discriminating: assert the base class ReasoningError, so a transient/permanent/rate-limit misclassification still passes
findings.description: The whole purpose of the T3 drain classifier (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:491-502) is to map an SDK result error to the CORRECT subtype, because the retry executor branches on the exact subtype: PermanentReasoningError => no retry (seam.py:599), RateLimitError => long backpressure (seam.py:601), base ReasoningError => fast backoff (seam.py:606). Both PermanentReasoningError and RateLimitError subclass ReasoningError (seam.py:66,70,74). Three drain tests assert the classification but only via pytest.raises(ReasoningError) (the base class): test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient (line 143-146), test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient (a 500, line 168-171), and test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient (line 202-206). Because pytest.raises catches subclasses, these pass even if the code misclassifies — e.g. a regression that mapped a generic 500 to PermanentReasoningError (don't retry) or RateLimitError would still satisfy pytest.raises(ReasoningError). The transient-vs-permanent distinction these tests claim to guard is exactly what is NOT asserted, so reverting the 'fall through to base ReasoningError' branch to a permanent/rate-limit classification would not be caught. By contrast, the bulk-path classification tests in test_reasoning.py (e.g. test_bulk_complete_classifies_404_as_permanent_by_status:382, test_malformed_params_raise_typed_permanent_error:404) correctly assert the SPECIFIC subtype, confirming this is an isolated weakness rather than an intentional convention. The empty-reply and mid-stream-raise cases are genuinely meant to be the base/transient type (retryable), so the assertion should pin that they are NOT the more-specific subtypes.
findings.poc: In src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py replace the generic fall-through at line 498 (`raise ReasoningError(...)`) with `raise PermanentReasoningError(...)`. This is a real misclassification: a transient 500 would now be treated as non-retryable. Re-run tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py::test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient — it still PASSES because PermanentReasoningError is a subclass of ReasoningError. The test fails to detect that the 500 is no longer retried.
findings.suggested_fix:
Make the transient assertions discriminating by excluding the more-specific subtypes. For each of the three tests, after capturing the exception assert the exact transient type, e.g.:

    with pytest.raises(ReasoningError) as ei:
        _run()
    assert type(ei.value) is ReasoningError  # exactly transient, not Permanent/RateLimit
    assert not isinstance(ei.value, (PermanentReasoningError, RateLimitError))

(or add a dedicated TransientReasoningError subclass and assert it). This makes a 500->permanent or empty->permanent regression fail every run, matching the discrimination already used on the bulk path.

findings.title: CV-O-1's strict decode silently REVERTS scrubbing for any transcript with one invalid UTF-8 byte — re-leaking the exact secrets/PII it was meant to redact
findings.description: CV-O-1 replaced the per-suffix gate with a decode-strict classifier: `try: text = raw.decode("utf-8")` then scrub, `except UnicodeDecodeError: data = raw` (copy verbatim). This introduces a follow-on regression of CV-O-1's own goal and of every prior scrubbing fix (#F-024/#F-051/#G-007/#H-018). PRE-fix, .jsonl files were decoded with `errors="replace"`, so they were ALWAYS scrubbed regardless of any malformed byte — only the single invalid byte became U+FFFD, the rest of the file (and all secrets/PII in it) was still redacted. POST-fix, a STRICT decode means a single invalid UTF-8 byte anywhere in a .jsonl/.json transcript raises UnicodeDecodeError and routes the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch, copying it byte-for-byte with NO scrubbing. Claude tool-result transcripts routinely embed arbitrary subprocess stdout (hexdumps, non-UTF-8/latin-1 file reads, truncated multibyte from a killed process) inside the JSONL string values, so this is a common, not exotic, condition. The net effect: the highest-severity privacy guarantee (no operator email/username/path + no credential-shaped secret in committed transcripts) now fails open precisely for the files most likely to contain raw dumped data. This is the #H-001 -> #CV-M-1 class the audit targets: the new code is the defect.
findings.poc:
import importlib.util, pathlib, tempfile
spec=importlib.util.spec_from_file_location('cap','scripts/capture_transcripts.py')
cap=importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(cap)
src=pathlib.Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())/'sess.jsonl'
# realistic: a tool-result string containing a secret + PII + one latin-1 byte (0x80) from subprocess output
src.write_bytes(b'{"out":"[REDACTED] caf\xe9byte=\x80 /Users/[REDACTED]/x"}')
raw=src.read_bytes()
try: text=raw.decode('utf-8'); data=cap._scrub(text).encode('utf-8'); branch='scrub'
except UnicodeDecodeError: data=raw; branch='verbatim'
print(branch)                       # -> verbatim
print(b'[REDACTED]' in data)      # -> True  (secret LEAKED)
print(b'[REDACTED]' in data)       # -> True  (username LEAKED)
print(b'REDACTED' in data)          # -> False (nothing scrubbed)
# Under the pre-CV-O-1 code this same .jsonl was decoded with errors='replace' and fully scrubbed.
findings.suggested_fix: Decode for SCRUBBING with errors='replace' (or 'surrogatepass'/'backslashreplace') instead of strict, so a stray byte never disables redaction; use the decode only to scrub, not to classify text-vs-binary. If a genuine binary verbatim path is still wanted, gate it on a real binary signal (e.g. a NUL byte: `if b"\x00" in raw: data = raw`) rather than on UTF-8 validity, since UTF-8-invalidity != binary and is common in legitimate text transcripts. Concretely: `text = raw.decode('utf-8', errors='replace'); data = _scrub(text).encode('utf-8')` for all non-NUL files, mirroring the prior .jsonl behavior but now applied to every file. Add a regression test: a .jsonl containing a secret AND an invalid byte must still come out scrubbed.

result.reasoning: Confirmed accurate. .github/workflows/ci.yml:193 runs `pip-licenses --format=json --with-system`, and `--with-system` enumerates EVERY distribution in site-packages — including pip/setuptools/wheel, the editable `-e .` install (line 191), and pip-licenses==5.5.5 plus its transitive deps (prettytable, wcwidth, etc.) installed at line 192. The downstream consumer scripts/license_gate.py does NO filtering: it iterates `for pkg in packages` over the entire JSON and even reports `len(packages)` as the count "checked." So the license-checked set is a strict superset of requirements.lock, directly contradicting the comment at lines 187-189 ("Scan the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited"). A copyleft license in any tooling/transitive dep would spuriously fail (or be misattributed to the distributed set). The secondary claim also holds: line 192 installs pip-licenses==5.5.5 WITHOUT `--require-hashes`, unlike line 190 and the rest of the job — a pinning inconsistency. I could not disprove any part of the finding.

Confirmed from code; could not disprove. Class hierarchy at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:66,70,74 shows PermanentReasoningError(66->70) and RateLimitError(74) both subclass ReasoningError. The retry executor branches on exact subtype: PermanentReasoningError -> no retry (seam.py:599), RateLimitError -> long backpressure (seam.py:601-604), base ReasoningError -> fast backoff (seam.py:606-609). Three drain tests assert only pytest.raises(ReasoningError), the base class: test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient (test_reasoning_drain.py:143-146), test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient (a 500; 168-171), test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient (202-206). Since pytest.raises catches subclasses, these pass even under misclassification. PoC verified by inspection: changing seam.py:498 from `raise ReasoningError(...)` to `raise PermanentReasoningError(...)` (a real regression making a transient 500 non-retryable) still satisfies pytest.raises(ReasoningError) in test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient, so the transient-vs-permanent distinction the test names is not actually guarded. The contrast is real: sibling tests in the SAME file assert the specific subtype where it matters (test_t3_auth_error_maps_to_permanent:164 -> PermanentReasoningError, test_t3_overloaded_529_maps_to_ratelimit:184 and test_t3_rate_limit_event_rejected_maps_to_ratelimit:151 -> RateLimitError), and the cited bulk-path tests in test_reasoning.py assert specific subtypes too — confirming an isolated weakness, not an intentional convention. The three transient cases are genuinely meant to be retryable base/transient errors, so the assertions should additionally pin that they are NOT PermanentReasoningError / RateLimitError. No mitigating mechanism elsewhere catches this. Finding stands.

Verified and could not disprove. The scrubber in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py redacts the operator email and the bare lowercase username handle ('[REDACTED]', via _USERNAME_RE = re.compile('[REDACTED]')) but does NOT redact the spaced full display name '[REDACTED]'. Direct PoC: _scrub("Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>") returns 'Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>' — email masked, full name leaks. grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/ shows it in cleartext in 20+ committed files, including the top-level session .jsonl (count 5) and many subagent/workflow transcripts, frequently adjacent to a successfully-redacted email. git log shows commit author '[REDACTED] <...>', confirming it is operator PII, not a coincidental token. No test in tests/test_scripts.py covers display-name redaction (line 88 only tests the concatenated handle, not the spaced name), confirming the gap is unintentional. This is the same coverage-gap class as pass-6 CV-O-1. Minor correction to the finding text: pyproject.toml:13 actually reads authors = [{ name = "[REDACTED]" }], not '[REDACTED]' — so that one example is inaccurate, but it does not affect the core finding, which is independently demonstrated via git author strings and transcript prose. The leak is real and committed.

Verified against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py. _scrub() is invoked exactly once — line 113, on file CONTENT inside _copy_scrubbed. The MANIFEST write block (lines 151-166) serializes the dict via json.dumps() with no _scrub() call on any field. Confirmed bypassing fields: (1) active_session = sid (line 157/134), where sid = session.stem — a source filename, never scrubbed; (2) captured[].path (line 119) = str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)), where dstf is built from sid and rel (= f.relative_to(subtree)), i.e. derived from source filenames, never scrubbed. _copy_scrubbed scrubs the bytes written to disk but returns the RAW relative-path string into the manifest record — exactly the contrast the finding describes vs CV-O-1.

The LOW severity is correctly justified: I inspected transcripts/MANIFEST.json and transcripts/sessions/ — all current stems are opaque UUIDs (8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d) and agent ids (agent-a05067d59cf434caf), so nothing leaks in practice today. I tried to disprove the finding by searching for any alternate scrubbing path for the manifest strings or any constraint guaranteeing PII-free filenames; neither exists. The structural claim — a committed artifact (MANIFEST) whose path/session fields bypass the redaction path that the file content goes through — is accurate and unrefuted. The PoC matches the code exactly. Finding survives.

CONFIRMED — could not disprove. Code at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:108-113 matches the finding verbatim: `try: text = raw.decode("utf-8") ... except UnicodeDecodeError: data = raw` (raw copied with NO scrubbing). The PoC reproduces exactly: a .jsonl byte string containing `[REDACTED]...`, `caf\xe9`, a single invalid latin-1 byte `\x80`, and `/Users/[REDACTED]/x` raises UnicodeDecodeError under strict decode, routing the entire file to the verbatim branch → branch=verbatim, secret leaked=True, username leaked=True, REDACTED present=False. Git history confirms the regression is CV-O-1's own change: commit e3eb708 (hardening-p6, "CV-O-1") replaced `data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")` with the strict `decode("utf-8")` + verbatim-on-error pattern. I directly verified the PRE-fix `errors="replace"` path on the identical bytes: secret leaked=False, username leaked=False, REDACTED present=True, and _USERNAME_RE is active — i.e., the old code fully scrubbed exactly what the new code leaks. The defect is fail-open precisely for transcripts most likely to embed non-UTF-8 subprocess stdout (hexdumps, latin-1 file reads, truncated multibyte), which Claude tool-result JSONL routinely carries. While CV-O-1 correctly broadened scrubbing to .json files (a genuine improvement), it simultaneously swapped lossy decoding for strict decoding, re-leaking secrets/PII (#F-024/#F-051/#G-007/#H-018 class) for any transcript containing a single invalid UTF-8 byte. Fix: restore `errors="replace"` (or scrub the latin-1/lossy-decoded text) instead of copying raw bytes verbatim. File: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py:108-113.

Verified against the code; could not disprove. tracking.py:64-68 _experiment_id() is an unguarded check-then-create TOCTOU: get_experiment_by_name() then, if None, create_experiment() — no exception handling, no idempotency lock. start_run (line 72) calls _experiment_id(client) BEFORE the try/except block (lines 78-86) that protects only log_param via #F-023 termination; the create_experiment call is entirely outside any guard, so its exception propagates straight out of start_run. The consumer in pipeline.py:351-370 catches any start_run exception and leaves run_id=None, after which the code's own comment (line 361) confirms "every track() call silently no-ops and ALL metrics are dropped for the whole run" — no MLflow run is created at all. Reachability is real: the module is designed for a local shared store (sqlite:///assay_mlflow.db, _DEFAULT_URI line 17) and MlflowClient is recreated per call (_client, line 62), so concurrent first-time start_run calls across the engine's thread pool / multiple study processes legitimately race the create. The duplicate create is rejected by MLflow's documented experiment-name uniqueness constraint (MlflowException RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS) in both backends. No test exercises this path: the only mock (test_observability.py:140-144) returns None from get_experiment_by_name and a non-raising create_experiment, so the duplicate-create branch is untested. The PoC's logic is sound. The one assumption I could not execute locally (mlflow not installed) is MLflow's duplicate-name rejection, but that is standard, documented MLflow behavior and the code provides zero defense against it (no try/except, no retry-on-conflict re-lookup, no advisory lock — unlike the persistence layer which does use advisory-locked one-time setup per its tests). Finding stands.

I attempted to disprove the finding and could not. All load-bearing claims verified against the code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

1. CI workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml) installs the package with `pip install --no-deps -e .` in all three jobs: core (line 35), integration (line 80), and audit/license-gate (line 191). Confirmed by reading the file.

2. pyproject.toml lines 1-3 declare `build-system.requires = ["hatchling"]` with `build-backend = "hatchling.build"`, and the version is dynamic (`dynamic = ["version"]` line 7, sourced via `[tool.hatch.version]` path = src/assay_engine/__init__.py, lines 76-77). A dynamic-version editable install forces pip to invoke the build backend to generate metadata.

3. The technical core is correct standard pip behavior: `--no-deps` suppresses only RUNTIME dependency resolution; it does NOT disable PEP 517 build isolation. Disabling build isolation requires `--no-build-isolation`. I grepped the entire .github/ tree and the whole repo (excluding transcripts) and found NO `--no-build-isolation`, no `PIP_NO_BUILD_ISOLATION`, no `build-isolation` reference, and no pip.conf/pip.ini anywhere. Therefore build isolation is ON (the default), and pip creates an isolated env that fetches `hatchling` plus its transitive build deps (editables, trove-classifiers, pathspec, packaging, pluggy) LIVE from PyPI, un-hashed, on every CI run. `--require-hashes` from the `-r ...lock` install does not propagate into the PEP 517 build environment.

4. Verified the PoC grep: `grep -ic '^hatchling==' requirements.lock requirements-core.lock` returns 0 for both; `editables` and `trove-classifiers` also return 0 for both. So the build toolchain is genuinely absent from both lockfiles.

5. Consequences hold: pip-audit runs only `-r requirements.lock` (line 153) and the SBOM is generated from the same lockfile (line 173), so the build-time dependency set is never scanned, never hash-pinned, and never in the SBOM. A compromised hatchling/editables patch release would execute arbitrary code during the editable build in all three jobs yet evade the scan — exactly the #F-004 supply-chain window the comments (lines 29-32, 76-77) claim is closed. A yanked/changed hatchling release also breaks reproducibility of a previously-green commit.

No mitigation found (no pre-installed pinned hatchling, no build-constraints file, no --no-build-isolation, no pip config). The finding is accurate and survives.

Minor scoping note (does not defeat the finding): the lockfile-sync gate (lines 124-138) regenerates locks via `uv pip compile`, which also does not include build-backend deps, so the gate would not surface this either. The fix would be to either pin/hash the build toolchain (e.g., a build-constraints/build-requirements lock fed to the build env) or use `--no-build-isolation` with a hash-pinned, pre-installed, audited hatchling.

deduplicated_findings.title: Strict UTF-8 decode in scrubber fails OPEN: one invalid byte routes the whole transcript to verbatim copy, re-leaking secrets/PII
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. _copy_scrubbed() does `try: text = raw.decode('utf-8') except UnicodeDecodeError: data = raw`. A single invalid UTF-8 byte anywhere in a .jsonl/.json transcript raises UnicodeDecodeError and routes the ENTIRE file to the verbatim branch, copied byte-for-byte with no scrubbing. Claude tool-result transcripts routinely embed arbitrary subprocess stdout (hexdumps, latin-1/non-UTF-8 file reads, truncated multibyte from killed processes) inside JSONL string values, so this is a common condition. Pre-CV-O-1 the .jsonl path decoded with errors='replace' and was ALWAYS scrubbed; the CV-O-1 fix turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one precisely for the files most likely to carry raw dumped data. This is the new code being the defect — the highest-severity privacy guarantee (no operator email/username/path, no credential-shaped secret) now silently fails for affected files.
deduplicated_findings.poc: src.write_bytes(b'{"out":"[REDACTED] caf\xe9byte=\x80 /Users/[REDACTED]/x"}'); raw=src.read_bytes(); try: data=_scrub(raw.decode('utf-8')).encode() except UnicodeDecodeError: data=raw  ->  b'[REDACTED]' in data == True (LEAKED), b'[REDACTED]' in data == True (LEAKED), b'REDACTED' in data == False. Under pre-CV-O-1 code the same file was decoded errors='replace' and fully scrubbed.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Decode for scrubbing with errors='replace' (or 'backslashreplace'/'surrogatepass') so a stray byte never disables redaction: `text = raw.decode('utf-8', errors='replace'); data = _scrub(text).encode('utf-8')`. If a genuine binary verbatim path is still wanted, gate it on a real binary signal (`if b'\x00' in raw: data = raw`) rather than on UTF-8 validity, since UTF-8-invalidity != binary. Add a regression test: a .jsonl containing a secret AND an invalid byte must come out scrubbed.

deduplicated_findings.title: Operator full display name ('[REDACTED]') is not scrubbed — leaks in 252 committed transcript files alongside redacted emails
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. _SECRET_PATTERNS + _USERNAME_RE redact the operator's email and the bare home-dir username ('[REDACTED]'), and mask the username inside paths/slugs, but do NOT redact the operator's real full DISPLAY NAME ('[REDACTED]'), which is distinct operator PII appearing via git author strings, pyproject authors=[], and prose. Confirmed live and already committed: `grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/` returns 252 files with the name in cleartext, frequently directly beside a successfully-redacted email (e.g. `Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>`). `git log -1 --format=%an` returns '[REDACTED]', confirming this is the operator identity, not a coincidental token. De-identifying the email while leaving the adjacent full name defeats the de-identification purpose of the #F-024/#G-007 work. No test in tests/test_scripts.py covers full-name redaction (0 matches), confirming an unintentional gap rather than a documented scope decision.
deduplicated_findings.poc: 1) cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay  2) git log -1 --format='%an' -> '[REDACTED]' (operator PII)  3) grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/ -> 252 files with count >=1 (cleartext)  4) _scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <a@b.c>') -> 'Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>' (email masked, full name leaks).
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Derive the operator display name (from `git config user.name` and/or a configurable ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES env list) and add it to the redaction set, mirroring _username_pattern(): re.escape'd, word-boundaried, length-guarded, applied in _scrub() (skip if empty/too-short to avoid over-redacting common words). Add a test_scripts.py case asserting _scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <a@b.c>') masks BOTH name and email. Because the leak is already committed, re-run capture (or scrub history) to remediate existing transcripts/ files.
deduplicated_findings.dimension: privacy / operator PII (coverage gap, CV-O-1 class)

deduplicated_findings.title: Build backend (hatchling) fetched live and un-hashed during `pip install -e .`, defeating the hash-pin/reproducibility guarantee
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. All three jobs install with `pip install --no-deps -e .` (lines 35, 80, 191). `--no-deps` suppresses runtime deps but does NOT disable PEP 517 build isolation. pyproject.toml declares `build-system.requires = ['hatchling']` and uses hatchling.build (confirmed), and version is dynamic, so pip creates an isolated build env and fetches hatchling + its deps (editables, trove-classifiers) LIVE from PyPI, un-pinned and un-hashed, every CI run. Neither lockfile contains hatchling (`grep -ic '^hatchling==' requirements.lock requirements-core.lock` -> 0/0; 0 matches for any 'hatchling'). Consequences: (1) the workflow's central claim of installing 'EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned' is false for the build toolchain; (2) pip-audit (-r requirements.lock) never sees hatchling, so a compromised hatchling/editables patch executes arbitrary code during the editable build yet is never scanned — the exact #F-004 window the comments claim closed; (3) the SBOM omits the build-time dep set; (4) a yanked/changed hatchling makes a green commit non-reproducible.
deduplicated_findings.poc: grep -ic '^hatchling==' requirements.lock requirements-core.lock -> 0 and 0, while the package still builds via hatchling.build. On a clean runner `pip install --no-deps -e .` emits 'Installing build dependencies: hatchling, editables, ...' resolved live from PyPI with no --require-hashes and no lock entry.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Include the build backend in the hashed set and disable live build isolation in CI: (a) add hatchling to a build/dev group, regenerate locks with --generate-hashes, and install with `pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` so the hashed hatchling is used; or (b) build a wheel in a separately-pinned hashed build env and pip-install that wheel. Extend SBOM/pip-audit coverage to the build toolchain.

deduplicated_findings.title: Check-then-create TOCTOU in _experiment_id loses the whole MLflow run under concurrent first-time start_run
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. _experiment_id() does get_experiment_by_name() then, if None, create_experiment() — a classic TOCTOU (lines 64-68). The first time a given experiment name is used, two concurrent start_run() calls can both see None and both call create_experiment(); MLflow rejects the loser with MlflowException(RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS), which propagates out of _experiment_id -> start_run. Reachable under the engine's own concurrency model: the reasoning seam runs an 8-thread pool, and multiple study processes legitimately share one local store. The consequence is exactly the lifecycle failure the rest of the module is engineered to avoid: in run_study a start_run exception leaves run_id=None, after which every track() call silently no-ops, dropping ALL metrics, the run_duration SLO metric, and provenance-trail artifact correlation for that entire study — and no MLflow run exists. The module's #F-023 orphan-termination logic targets phantom runs, but the experiment-creation step itself is unguarded, and no test covers the duplicate-create path.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Two threads call tracker.start_run('r', {}) for a brand-new experiment name (get_experiment_by_name == None for both); both enter create_experiment, one raises MlflowException(RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS). In run_study this surfaces as run_id=None -> all metrics for the study silently dropped.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Make resolution race-tolerant: wrap create_experiment in try/except that, on the duplicate-name error (RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS, or any failure), re-fetches via get_experiment_by_name(self._experiment) and returns its experiment_id; re-raise only if the re-fetch still returns None. Mirrors mlflow.set_experiment's get-or-create idiom. Add a test simulating create_experiment raising already-exists and asserting fallback.

deduplicated_findings.title: T3 transient error-classification tests are non-discriminating: assert base ReasoningError, so a transient->permanent/rate-limit misclassification still passes
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. The T3 drain classifier's purpose is to map an SDK error to the CORRECT subtype because the retry executor branches on exact subtype (PermanentReasoningError => no retry; RateLimitError => long backpressure; base ReasoningError => fast backoff). Both subtypes subclass ReasoningError. Three drain tests assert classification only via pytest.raises(ReasoningError) (the base class): test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient (145), test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient (a 500, 170), test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient (205). Since pytest.raises catches subclasses, these pass even if the code misclassifies — e.g. mapping a generic 500 to PermanentReasoningError (don't retry) or RateLimitError still satisfies the assertion. The transient-vs-permanent distinction these tests claim to guard is exactly what is NOT asserted. By contrast, the same file's specific tests correctly pin subtypes (test_t3_auth_error_maps_to_permanent:164 -> PermanentReasoningError; test_t3_overloaded_529_maps_to_ratelimit:182 -> RateLimitError), confirming an isolated weakness, not a convention.
deduplicated_findings.poc: In seam.py replace the generic fall-through `raise ReasoningError(...)` with `raise PermanentReasoningError(...)` (a real misclassification: a transient 500 becomes non-retryable). Re-run test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient — it still PASSES because PermanentReasoningError subclasses ReasoningError; the test fails to detect the 500 is no longer retried.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Make the transient assertions discriminating: capture the exception and assert it is exactly the base/transient type and not the specific subtypes, e.g. `with pytest.raises(ReasoningError) as ei: _run(); assert type(ei.value) is ReasoningError; assert not isinstance(ei.value, (PermanentReasoningError, RateLimitError))` (or introduce a dedicated TransientReasoningError and assert it). Apply to all three tests so a transient->permanent/rate-limit regression fails every run.

deduplicated_findings.title: License gate scans entire site-packages (--with-system), not the hash-pinned set the comment claims
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. The license-gate comment asserts it scans 'the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited.' In practice the command is `pip-licenses --format=json --with-system` (line 193), enumerating EVERY distribution in site-packages. By that point the env also contains pip-licenses==5.5.5 and its transitive deps (prettytable, wcwidth), plus pip/setuptools/wheel via --with-system, plus the live-fetched build backend. So the license-checked set is a superset of requirements.lock, contradicting the comment and coupling the production-distribution license verdict to dev/tooling packages — a copyleft license in a tool dep would spuriously fail the gate. Additionally pip-licenses==5.5.5 is version-pinned but installed without --require-hashes (line 192), unlike every other install in the job — a minor pinning inconsistency.
deduplicated_findings.poc: `pip-licenses --with-system` output set != requirements.lock package set; it includes pip-licenses, prettytable, wcwidth, pip, setuptools, wheel, and the build backend, none of which are in requirements.lock.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Scan only the pinned distribution set: run pip-licenses without --with-system against the locked env, or drive it from requirements.lock package names. Install pip-licenses in a separate step/venv so tooling deps never enter the scanned environment, and add --require-hashes for the pip-licenses install to match the rest of the job.

deduplicated_findings.title: MANIFEST.json file paths and active_session are written without passing through _scrub
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. main() writes transcripts/MANIFEST.json via json.dumps() with no scrubbing; each entry's 'path' is str(dstf.relative_to(...)) (line 119) and the top-level 'active_session' is the session stem (line 158), derived from source filenames. Today all captured filenames are opaque UUID/agent-id stems with no PII, so nothing leaks in practice — hence LOW. But it is the same structural class as CV-O-1: a committed artifact (the manifest) that bypasses the redaction path entirely. If any future transcript file in the session subtree is ever named with operator-identifying content (a path/slug/username in a filename), that PII would be copied verbatim into MANIFEST.json. File CONTENT is scrubbed; the manifest of paths is not.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Inspect main(): the dict (captured[].path + active_session) is serialized with json.dumps() and written to MANIFEST without any _scrub() call; _copy_scrubbed scrubs file content but returns the raw relative path string for the manifest.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Run the manifest's string fields through _scrub() before writing (scrub each rec['path'] and 'active_session', or _scrub the json.dumps blob before write_text). Cheap defense-in-depth that closes the structural gap for future filename shapes.
